prompt: Is it ever ethical to lie to someone you love? Answer in prose, taking a position.
response: Yes—sometimes it is not only ethical but an act of love to withhold or even distort the truth. The key is to ask whose burden the truth will be, and what it will cost the beloved. If a lie is told to spare suffering that the teller can quietly carry alone, and if the lie does not corrode the larger trust between two people, then it is morally permissible. When my grandmother was dying, her children told her that the doctors were “optimistic,” though the oncologist had privately given her weeks. That small fiction let her spend her last lucid days planning a garden she would never plant, laughing at memories instead of counting heartbeats. The lie did not change the outcome; it only changed the texture of her remaining hours. Had they spoken the bald prognosis, her final days would have belonged to fear, not to love. The children chose to bear the weight of the truth themselves, and in doing so they honored her more deeply than brutal honesty ever could. Love is not merely the obligation to speak facts; it is the obligation to protect the beloved’s flourishing when the facts themselves can do nothing but wound.
